hypotheses: Presentation of an auditory alerting cue increases visual processing speed.
The intensity of alerting should correlate with the observed estimates of processing speed.
claim.hypotheses_location: Introduction, paragraph concluding Section 1 (just before '2. Theory of Visual Attention').
claim.statement: Results show that phasic auditory alertness affects visual perception by increasing the visual processing speed and lowering the threshold of conscious perception (Experiment 1).
claim.statement_location: Abstract (first page): sentence beginning 'Results show that phasic auditory alertness affects visual perception...'.

data.source: Behavioral single-letter recognition task (accuracy-based); pupillometry (Eyelink 1000) for Experiment 3.
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 28 participants; Experiment 2: 28 participants (one excluded); Experiment 3: 25 participants for behavioral analysis (one excluded), pupillometry analyses: 24 participants (one additional technical exclusion).
data.access_details: Supplementary data associated with the article available online (referenced in paper). No direct public dataset repository or code link provided in the paper.
data.notes: Design details: cue intensities used were 85 dB and 40 dB; exposure durations: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 ms; target letters from a 20-letter set; non-aging (geometric) distributions used for ITI and CTOA; pupillometry data converted from pixels to mm using a model pupil and preprocessed with linear interpolation and artifact removal thresholds (samples <1 mm or gradients >0.075 mm flagged); trial counts per participant reported (e.g., Experiment 1: 960 trials per participant across 2 sessions).

method.description: Across three experiments participants performed a single-letter recognition task with backward masking across varied exposure durations. Trials included either no auditory cue or an auditory alerting cue (85 dB in Experiment 1; 40 dB and 85 dB conditions in Experiments 2 and 3). TVA-based modeling was applied to per-subject accuracy vs exposure duration data to estimate visual processing speed (v) and perceptual threshold (t0). Experiment 3 additionally recorded pupil diameter with an Eyelink 1000 to index physiological alertness.
steps: Screen participants for normal or corrected-to-normal vision and normal hearing; obtain consent and ethics approval.
Set up stimuli: fixation cross, single target letter presented left or right of fixation, backward masks at both locations; letters from 20-letter set; letters and masks equiluminant in Experiment 3.
Implement trial timing with non-aging (geometric) ITI and CTOA distributions (specified hazard rates and step sizes) to reduce temporal expectancy.
In cue trials present auditory cue (500 Hz or 900 Hz pitch, balanced) at specified intensity (85 dB in Exp1; 40 dB and 85 dB in Exp2/3) preceding target by variable CTOA.

Present target letter for one of eight exposure durations, followed immediately by pattern masks for 500 ms; participant reports identity non-speeded if 'fairly certain', otherwise skips to next trial.
Provide feedback after each trial; run designated number of trials/sessions (e.g., Experiment 1: 960 trials per participant).

For each participant and condition, fit TVA model (including guessing parameter pg) to accuracy-by-duration data via maximum-likelihood estimation using the Nelder-Mead simplex algorithm to estimate v and t0.
Perform statistical comparisons (paired t tests) across conditions for TVA parameters; conduct likelihood-ratio model comparisons when appropriate.

In Experiment 3, record pupil diameter at 250 Hz (left eye) with Eyelink 1000, convert pixels to mm via model pupil, remove blinks/artifacts via interpolation and amplitude/gradient thresholds, exclude trials with artifactual samples in critical windows, extract peak dilations in early and late windows and correlate with TVA estimates.
method.models: Bundesen's Theory of Visual Attention (TVA) used to model encoding probability as function of exposure duration. Parameters estimated: processing speed (v), perceptual threshold (t0), and guessing probability (pg). Maximum-likelihood estimation (Nelder-Mead) in Matlab. Model comparisons via likelihood-ratio tests. Neural interpretation (NTVA) discussed.
method.outcome_variable: Primary behavioral outcomes: probability of correct letter report by exposure duration; derived TVA parameters: processing speed (v, letters/s) and perceptual threshold (t0, ms). Physiological outcome: pupil diameter (mm) and peak dilation measures.
method.independent_variables: Experimental manipulations: auditory alerting cue presence (no cue vs cue) and intensity (40 dB vs 85 dB), exposure duration of letter, cue pitch (500 vs 900 Hz, balanced), target location (left vs right).
method.control_variables: Balanced cue pitches to avoid habituation; letters presented equally often at both locations; masks presented at both locations to reduce eye movements; non-aging ITI and CTOA distributions to reduce temporal expectancy; screening for normal vision/hearing; exclusion of participants failing instructions or with excessive guessing; equiluminant stimuli in Experiment 3 to avoid luminance-driven pupil changes.
method.tools_software: Stimulus presentation: E-Prime 2. Modeling and statistical analyses: Matlab (maximum-likelihood estimation using Nelder-Mead simplex). Pupillometry: Eyelink 1000 (SR Research). Custom preprocessing scripts (linear interpolation), references to Murphy et al. (2014) methods.

results.summary: Across three experiments, presentation of a phasic auditory alerting cue increased visual processing speed (v) and lowered the perceptual threshold (t0) compared with no-cue trials. Increasing cue intensity (40 dB to 85 dB) produced further increases in processing speed, whereas perceptual threshold was reduced by the presence of a cue but did not differ reliably between 40 dB and 85 dB. Experiment 3 replicated behavioral findings and showed that pupil dilation increased with cue presence and intensity; peak pupil dilation correlated positively with processing speed for near-threshold exposure durations.

metadata.original_paper_title: The effect of phasic auditory alerting on visual perception
metadata.original_paper_data: Supplementary data associated with the article available online (referenced in paper at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2017.04.004).
